data.title: The Reason Why Intuit Is A Quality Fortress You Shouldn't Invest In (Yet)
data.description:
Intuit (NASDAQ:INTU) is undoubtedly a high-quality business that boasts a subscription-based business model with an "ecosystem effect" that creates deep defensive moats through its QuickBooks and TurboTax platforms. The company boasts good financial health, solid growth, exceptional free cash flow margins, and minuscule debt.

However, despite these strengths, its current valuation is demanding and discounts perfection in the execution of its strategy. The decline in its return on invested capital (ROIC) in recent years, its high stock-based compensation, and the competitive threat from the IRS's Direct File program make me cautious about the price to pay for the company. Although the AI ​​strategy is promising and ambitious, the current risk/reward balance is not sufficiently attractive in my opinion.

Investment thesis

The business model of Intuit is based on recurrent software subscriptions. The company has created an ecosystem where its four segments (QuickBooks, Turbotax, Credit Karma and Mailchimp) interact and reinforce each other. In my opinion, this integration creates a differentiation compared to peers, as it allows clients to handle their accounting, financing, taxes, and marketing in a single place. This is even more relevant for Intuit, as most of its clients are small- and mid-cap companies, where simplifying IT infrastructure and optimizing costs are a must. Therefore, such an offering creates a solid client base that generates recurrent revenues for the company and attracts new customers into the business, fostering a network effect.

Such level of adoption allows the company to collect immense amounts of data from customers, which is used to understand their behaviour and which is the cornerstone of the AI strategy of the company, as this high-quality data from customers grants an edge to INTU that is difficult to be replicated by peers.

Nonetheless, the business has weaknesses as well that one must be aware of. The most remarkable bearish narrative relies on the high valuation multiples that basically discount a flawless execution of its strategy, which leaves no room for errors. We know what happens in the market when a company priced for perfection does not deliver, one could expect sharp corrections. Therefore, we need to be disciplined with the price to pay for the business. Moreover, another key question to answer about the company is the true risk that the IRS "Direct File" program represents to INTU's TurboTax segment. We will discuss this point later in the article.

Financial Health

Revenues have been growing in the mid-teens range. Also, it has experienced an operating margin expansion from around 21% in 2015 to 26% in 2025, and a net margin of around 20%.

Even more notable is the FCF margin, which has historically been around 30%, meaning that for every dollar of revenue, the company generates 30 cents of FCF. This is exceptional and demonstrates the high quality of the business.

Regarding its debt, the company financed the acquisitions of Credit Karma and Mailchimp in 2020 and 2021, respectively. Despite this, net debt stands at only $2,087 million, which implies a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 0.37.

Points of Caution

Despite its strengths, there are two major points of attention about the company's financial profile that require our attention:

  1. The decline in return on invested capital: Intuit has gone from having an ROIC significantly above 30% for many years to just 12% in the last 3 years. This is due to the acquisitions of Credit Karma and Mailchimp, which led to a massive investment that is not yet compensated by the operating profit they generate. Although it's normal for post-acquisition synergies to take time to materialize, Mailchimp's slow growth is something to consider when valuing the company and is the main reason why the market has recently penalized the stock price.

2. High Stock-Based Compensation: Stock-based compensation has gone from representing 6% of the company's total revenue 10 years ago, which is already quite high, to more than 10% (which, in my opinion, is a red flag).

Although the company aggressively repurchases shares, it merely avoids dilution instead of creating shareholder value that boosts EPS, as is the case with other companies in the sector.

Growth Catalysts and Risks

The company's potential growth, and therefore the success of the investment, depends on two factors in my opinion:

Monetization of the "AI-Driven Expert Platform":

A key pillar of the bull narrative for the company lies in transforming Intuit into a platform that uses AI to proactively manage its customers' finances. The success of this strategy is visible in the 47% growth in TurboTax Live revenue, a service that combines software with human expert assistance, and which is improving employee productivity. According to CEO Sasan Goodarzi:

Our data and AI platform capabilities had a profound impact on the productivity of our experts. By doing a lot of the work for them and helping them finish returns quickly and accurately, they spent about 20% less time preparing a return.

Therefore, it is important for investors to monitor the revenue growth of TurboTax Live and the ARPC of QuickBooks Online, both of which have re-accelerated in the last fiscal year.

If this trend continues, it would be a sign that customers are adopting additional plans and services, generating higher revenue per user, and that the company's competitive advantages are strengthening.

IRS "Direct File" program threat for the TurboTax franchise

TurboTax is a high-margin revenue source for Intuit. The biggest structural threat is the possibility of the U.S. government offering a free and direct tax-filing system. The problem is that, more than a threat, it seems to be a reality. The IRS pilot program is already available in 25 U.S. states. Following a successful initial pilot with over 140,000 participants who reported a high level of satisfaction, the expansion of this service is a risk that cannot be ignored.

The potential loss of customers who used the free version of TurboTax, or those with simpler tax returns, could have a negative impact on the monetization of those customers through adjacent ecosystem services like Credit Karma.

Although Intuit is working to specialize through the mix offered by TurboTax Live, combining software with human tax experts, the fact is that when valuing the company now, one must consider that a permanent competitor has appeared on the scene.

Valuation

I've applied a DCF model to measure the fair value of the company. For my estimates, I've been conservative in both the 5Y revenue growth assumption and the target EBITDA exit multiple with respect to market consensus. The reasons behind this decision are directly related to the points of concern mentioned previously.

From my standpoint, the low ROIC shown in recent years, which stands at a market average now, and the increased levels of stock-based compensation, make me believe that a premium EBITDA exit multiple with respect to peers is no longer deserved until we see a recovery on these metrics.

Therefore, assuming the following metrics:

Metric

Assumption/Calculation

Comment (Base-case)

5Y Revenue Growth (OTC:CAGR)

Base-case: 12%

Slightly below market consensus of 13.5%

Discount Factor (OTC:WACC)

Base Case: 9%

Target Exit EBITDA Multiple

Base Case: 18.0x

Below sector median of 19.4x EV/EBITDA TTM
  • Base Case: Fair value estimate would be 719 USD.
  • Conservative Case: 627 USD, assuming Exit EBITDA Multiple of 16x.

Considering the quality of the business and the investment risks mentioned previously, I apply a 20% margin of safety, implying a price target of 575 USD.

Base Case Scenario - Forecast Summary:

Conclusion

Intuit is a quality business trading at a valuation that, in my opinion, is not attractive enough to justify starting a position. The multiples at which it trades remain demanding despite the recent stock price decline, suggesting that an investment at this level may struggle to outperform the market.

Factors such as high stock-based compensation, the low ROIC of recent years, and the threat of the IRS Direct File make me demand a higher discount for the company that would justify investing in it and offer a sufficiently attractive potential return to compensate for these risks.

Despite the above, I like the business model and the strategy the company is pursuing with the AI-Driven Expert Platform, which has great potential if executed correctly.

For now, I am keeping INTU on my watchlist, waiting for a larger margin of safety to make the risk/reward balance more favorable for the investment.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: SPXU: Understanding The Risks Of A -3X Leveraged S&P 500 ETF
data.description:
Fast facts

The ProShares UltraPro Short S&P500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPXU) is a leveraged ETF with a factor of -3x launched on 6/23/2009 and is based on the S&P 500® Index, with an expense ratio of 0.90%. SPXU is a very liquid, albeit mid-size ETF, with $571 million of AUM (assets under management) and a 3-month average daily dollar volume of $363 million. About 64% of AUM changes hands every day on average, pointing to intensive use of the fund by day traders. The issuer ProShares specializes in leveraged and inverse ETFs, with about one hundred such products based on equity indexes, bond indexes, commodities, currencies and cryptos.

Strategy

SPXU’s objective is to provide, before fees and expenses, three times the inverse (-3x) of the daily performance of the S&P 500 Index. ProShares gives a warning on SPXU:

For any holding period other than a day, your return may be higher or lower than the Daily Target. These differences may be significant.

Indeed, the daily leverage factor generates drift, a topic that I will develop in another section of this article.

The fund invests in instruments that, in combination, provide -3x daily leveraged exposure to the Index, primarily swap agreements and futures contracts. The fund also holds cash instruments such as Treasury bills and repurchase agreements as collateral. Keeping a daily objective requires to rebalance the portfolio on a daily basis. The fund’s turnover rate on cash instruments and derivatives is not disclosed.

Some gains from derivatives may be paid as dividends, which explains why SPXU has a high 12-month trailing yield (8.42%). However, it is definitely not an income ETF and its performance should be considered based on total return. Distributions are paid quarterly.

This article will use SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) as a reference for the S&P 500 Index.

Underlying index

The S&P 500 is composed of 500 of the largest companies listed and domiciled in the U.S., weighted based on market capitalization. It is reconstituted annually in September and rebalanced quarterly in March, June, and December. The index is quite concentrated. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table based on SPY holdings, represent 40.2% of asset value, and the top three names (Nvidia, Apple and Microsoft) weigh between 6% and 8%.

SPXU's Portfolio

Besides cash and short-term Treasury securities, the fund holds:

  • E-Mini S&P 500 futures, with a notional weight of -12.5%.

The total of -300% corresponds to the leverage factor. Swaps are issued by UBS AG, Goldman Sachs, Societe Generale, JPMorgan Chase, Morgan Stanley, BNP Paribas, Barclays, Citibank and Bank Of America. Diversification in several issuers reduces counterparty risks and dependency.

Weights are given as an example from 10/22/2025 and may have changed by the time you read this.

Performance

SPXU has lost almost all of its value from 6/30/2009 to 10/22/2025 through multiple reverse splits, as reported in the next table. The ratio of annualized returns between SPXU and SPY in this time fame is -2.85, which is quite close to the daily leverage factor.

This ratio varies a lot from one time frame to another, suggesting that SPXU is subject to path-dependent drift.

Why and how do leveraged ETFs drift?

Leveraged ETFs often underperform their underlying index leveraged by the same factor. ETF decay may have four reasons: beta-slippage, roll yield, tracking errors, and management costs. Beta-slippage is the main reason for equity leveraged ETFs such as SPXU. To understand it, imagine a very volatile asset that goes up 25% one day and down 20% the day after. A perfect 2X leveraged ETF goes up 50% the first day and down 40% the second day. On the close of the second day, the underlying asset is back to its initial price:

(1 + 0.25) x (1 - 0.2) = 1

In the same time, the perfect leveraged ETF has lost 10%:

(1 + 0.5) x (1 - 0.4) = 0.9

It is just the result of compounding leveraged returns. In a trending market, beta-slippage can be positive. If the underlying index goes up 10% two days in a row, on the second day, it is up 21%:

(1 + 0.1) x (1 + 0.1) = 1.21

The perfect 2X leveraged ETFs is up 44%:

(1 + 0.2) x (1 + 0.2) = 1.44

Beta-slippage is path-dependent. If the underlying index gains 50% on day 1 and loses 33.33% on day 2, it is back to its initial value, like in the first example. However, the 2X ETF loses one third of its value, instead of 10% in the first case:

(1 + 1) x (1 - 0.6667) = 0.6667

With a demonstration, it shows that the higher the volatility, the larger the decay. Moreover, the higher the leverage factor, the larger the decay. If we come back to the first example, a perfect 3X ETF would gain 75% on day 1 and lose 60% on day 2:

(1 + 0.75) x (1 - 0.6) = 0.7

The 3X ETF is down 30% in two days, while the 2X ETF is down only 10% and the underlying index is unchanged. And the “perfect” -3X ETF?

(1 - 0.75) x (1 + 0.6) = 0.4

It is down 60%. This theoretical example suggest that -3X ETFs are very risky.

Drift calculation and history

There is no standard definition for the drift of a leveraged ETF. Mine is simple and based on the difference between the leveraged ETF performance and Ñ times the performance of the underlying index on a given time interval, if Ñ is the leveraging factor. First, let’s start by defining “Return”: it is the total return of a leveraged ETF in a given time interval. “Index Return” is the total return of a non-leveraged ETF on the same underlying asset in the same time interval. “Abs” is the absolute value operator. “Drift” is the drift of a leveraged ETF normalized to the underlying index exposure in a time interval, calculated as follows:

Drift = (Return - (Index Return x Ñ))/ Abs(Ñ)

The next table reports monthly and annual drifts of leveraged bull and bear ETFs based on the S&P 500.

SPXU has positive monthly and yearly drifts. Unfortunately, long-term data are less encouraging. The next chart plots the 12-month drift of SPXU from 1/1/2000, simulated with synthetic prices before inception.

The 12-month drift has a negative time-weighted average (-2.14%). Although SPXU may be used to hedge an equity portfolio against sharp downturns in an upward trending market, holding it over the long term results in significant decay when volatility kicks in. Additionally, the chart above shows that the fund may suffer from steep decay over quite long periods.

Competitor

SPXU has a direct competitor with the same daily objective: Direxion Daily S&P 500® Bear 3X Shares ETF (SPXS). The chart below compares their characteristics.

SPXU has twice the dollar trading volumes on average and slightly lower fees, making it a more compelling trading instrument.

Takeaway: who should consider SPXU?

SPXU is best-suited for traders executing strategies with clear entry and exit signals, and who understand the fund’s behavior behind the leverage factor. Based on historical data, holding SPXU over a long period of time is likely to generate negative drift. Investors willing to buy or hold SPXU should stick to an active trading strategy backtested in all market conditions.

This article answers these three main questions about SPXU:

  1. Is SPXU meant for short-term trading or long-term investing?
  2. Are there unique risks to investing in SPXU?
  3. What type of investor is SPXU best suited for?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Mercedes: China And E.U. Energy Risks Make It A Hold
data.description:
European carmakers are having a hard time this century. Mercedes (OTCPK:MBGAF) (OTCPK:MBGYY) is no exception. Its share price is barely over half of what it was at its all-time high reached almost two decades ago. What is worse, there are few positive trends that one can point to in any of its major markets, with mostly downside risk factors on the horizon.

There are also reasons to be optimistic about Mercedes' long-term potential. It has a strong brand name, and it has invested wisely in many respects.

However, I cannot feel confident in investing in its shares due to current risk factors, such as the rising probability of yet another energy crisis in Europe. There are also risks of retaliation against the company in China, as EU-China relations continue to sour. These issues need to be settled one way or another. For now, it remains a hold.

A Weak Quarterly Result And Warning Signs Of More To Come As Q3 Sales Data Come In

As the company's Q3 sales results came in, it looked like two of its major markets were struggling. Sales in China declined by 27% Y-O-Y, while sales in the US declined by 17% in the same period. This does not bode well for its next quarterly financial report, after it already experienced a weak performance in the Second Quarter of this year.

In the first half of the year, it saw a 9% decline in revenues, to 66.4 billion euros, and a 56% decline in net earnings, to 2.7 billion euros. Its profit margin is currently about 4%. Its earnings per share of 2.69 euros, or about $3, for the first half of the year, compared with its yearly dividend per share of $4.44, has me wondering whether or not the generous dividend that Mercedes currently offers has staying power, if financial results do not improve soon. Based on the latest sales data, an improvement in its financial results might not be forthcoming.

The meager returns of less than 30% in terms of share price appreciation over a period of 20 years highlight the importance of that generous dividend yield, currently at over 7%.

A Weak Quarterly Result And Warning Signs Of More To Come As Q3 Sales Data Come In

Mercedes has three main markets, namely Europe, the US, and China.

Of the three markets, Europe & China are by far the largest, but the US is also important for the company, and it is, in my view, where Mercedes still has hope of increasing its sales.

Sales In China Were Negatively Impacted By Competition

China's new vehicle market is still somewhat robust, even though it is showing signs of stalling out. Up until this decade, Mercedes car sales followed along with the trend, and it looked like China would be its most important market by far.

My estimate for the whole of 2025 is based on the latest 27% Y-O-Y decline that Mercedes just reported in the Chinese market, which is part of a broader trend that many European carmakers are experiencing in China. It seems that the era of European carmakers relying on the Chinese market to make up for weak growth in the domestic European market is coming to an end, and it has gone in reverse.

Looking beyond this year, the outlook is uncertain for Mercedes sales in China. On one hand, the trend of European carmakers being pushed out of the Chinese car market by domestic carmakers could continue at the current pace. On the other hand, China might actually want European carmakers to continue having a stake in the Chinese market, to use it as leverage if Europe tries to curtail surging EV sales in Europe that Chinese brands like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY) are experiencing. Mercedes will probably see continued downward pressure on Chinese sales, but the pace of decline will slow after this year.

The European Car Market Outlook Is Bleak For Mercedes, Based On A Weak Economy, Difficulties Related To The 2035 Combustion Engine Ban, And Chinese Carmakers Gaining Market Share

Europe is the second-largest market for Mercedes cars after China. It is officially its domestic market, but it makes up less than one-third of its total car sales. European car sales are yet to recover to 2019 levels, when just under 18 million vehicles were sold. Last year, there were just under 13 million vehicles sold on the continent. Mercedes was no exception to the trend of declining sales in Europe. It sold just over 1 million cars in total in 2019, versus 640,000 last year.

One of the main reasons why European car sales have fallen so far below levels from the last decade is that the European economy has been stagnant since 2020. A new energy crisis is potentially flying under the market's radar, with current natural gas inventories significantly below the five-year average.

So far, the markets have been remarkably calm over what seems to be a developing crisis, potentially becoming severe, depending on the weather this winter. If a shortfall occurs, it will most likely lead to yet another European economic downturn, which could have an outsized impact on an already struggling economy.

Aside from the struggling European economy, the 2035 ICE-vehicle sales ban is creating a situation where European automakers are ramping up EV sales, even as demand for them is not necessarily ever going to match that of ICE cars. In other words, as a result of forcing the European consumer market to go strictly electric in terms of personal vehicles, overall European sales are likely to decline significantly compared with sales volumes we saw before the start of this decade. I addressed this concept in an article I wrote a few years back for those who want to understand it more in-depth.

On top of the pressures that European carmakers, including Mercedes, are feeling from having to sell only EVs on the EU market by 2035, which I predict will lead to a much smaller overall car market, there is the rising tide of Chinese EV sales in Europe. When BYD starts producing its EVs in Hungary as early as the end of this year, its share of the European EV market can be expected to rise dramatically. It will also target the European market with production in Turkey and possibly Spain.

Mercedes is by no means looking to abandon its domestic market. It recently announced plans to double its production capacity at its Kecskemet factory in Hungary. The reason why this is newsworthy is that Hungary is emerging as the site where German luxury brands are pairing up with Asian EV battery makers to produce cost-competitive EVs for the European market. Hungary is set to be the world's 4th-largest EV battery producer by the end of the decade, so it makes sense for the likes of Mercedes as well as BMW (OTC:BMWKY), or Volkswagen's (OTCPK:VLKAF) Audi to center some of their EV manufacturing capacities in the country in Europe, where the batteries are also produced, making for cheaper and simpler logistics, as well as lower wages that Hungary has compared with Germany and other Western countries.

Mercedes Has Some Potential To Grow Its Presence In The Us Market, But It Is A Tough Environment For Luxury Carmakers

The EV pivot is not as prominent in the US vehicle market as it is in the European market, where it is more or less mandated by the 2035 deadline. However, in my view, EVs are eating into America's luxury car market. In other words, both the conventional luxury brand producers and EV producers are competing for the same upper-income bracket households. Tesla (TSLA) continues to lead US EV sales. The decline in Mercedes car sales in the US so far this year does not bode well for its future outlook in the US market; however, it is a market where, depending on consumer reactions to certain car concepts, anything could happen.

Investment Implications

Given the headwinds that I already identified for Mercedes, it is, in my view, not a bargain buy, even though it is trading at a share price that is roughly half compared with its all-time highs. Its forward P/E ratio of just over 10 may seem on the expensive side, but is more reasonable when compared with other carmakers. GM (GM), for instance, trades at a forward P/E ratio of just over 6, but it does not offer a notable dividend. However, GM is not faced with a possible massive hit this winter if Europe descends into another energy crisis, nor is its exposure to China as deep as Mercedes.

Mercedes is taking action to improve its competitiveness for the future, with its recent decision to expand its production capacity in Hungary being an obvious example. Average labor costs in Hungary are about 1/3 of what it costs to hire labor in Germany or many other Western European countries. It can also save on logistics by having suppliers for EV inputs, such as batteries, close by, being close to the source. However, at the moment, risk factors such as continued loss of market share in China or a potential energy crisis this winter in Europe are not factored into its current valuation; therefore, it is a hold until those factors either see some resolution or become priced in.

Risks To My Thesis

The risk of not buying Mercedes stock right now, but rather waiting for a more favorable price or for a resolution to potential downside risks, is that the company will see some signs of reversal in its fortunes in at least some or in all of its major markets, and its stock is going to rally on those signals. Given the sizable downside risks, I prefer to risk missing out, rather than risking a potentially losing investment.

A Resolution To At Least Some Of The Risks Can Signal A Time To Start Buying Mercedes Shares Incrementally

The main factor I am watching at the moment is whether or not the currently low natural gas storage levels in Europe will trigger a crisis this winter. So far, the month of October suggests that things are headed in the wrong direction, with the gap between current storage levels and the five-year average widening. Markets are yet to react to the trend, so perhaps there is something that investors are banking on to turn things around. If Europe's energy situation escalates in the next few months, there will most likely be a negative impact on the broader European markets; therefore, the risk becomes a reality that will be priced into Mercedes shares. Mercedes could then be a buying opportunity after the fact. If it turns out that Europe is safe from experiencing natural gas shortages or price spikes this winter, then the risk dissipates, and therefore it no longer needs to be factored into its share price.

Either way, it will be prudent to wait, perhaps until we get closer to the midpoint of the impending heating season, before I start buying Mercedes shares incrementally.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Is Retail Actually The Smart Money?
data.description:
I do a fair bit of institution-watching, that is, following the moves of institutional investors, asset managers, hedge funds, etc., or better known colloquially as "smart money."

Here is a quick primer from my last article on the subject:

...before we get further into this article...I encounter a slew of the same comments across all of these kinds of articles discussing institutional data.

First, the epithet of the group can be perceived as divisive, but I don't make the rules nor set monikers. The term itself is intended to articulate the sizable advantages institutional investors hold related to resources, tools, and formal expertise in the aggregate over retail investors. It is not intended to belittle retail investors' intelligence, although it is reasonable to expect that it comes across that way in many cases. If it bothers you, try thinking of it as "capital allocators" instead. Less catchy and fun to read, but just as accurate without all the bruised egos.

Second, there have been numerous studies done over the years that show the performance of institutions as it relates to how "smart money" performance is collected. There is no definitive source, as the returns of institutions vary by institution and often are measured differently by institutions than retail investors; e.g., the performance of hedge funds is often compared to the S&P 500, although they do not compare themselves that way because their goal is not to create equity-like returns...

Regardless of the pre-empting I did (there are several more paragraphs after those), the comments were still hung up on the same topic about whether "smart money" is right or correct or actually "smart" at all. I can't run away from it. Of course, not all comments were about that, but I felt like there was enough to say that the consensus of commenters (not necessarily readers, as I don't know their opinions if they didn't comment) was that smart money isn't smart at all.

The issue, as I raised in the quoted text above, is that there is no way to really quantify the performance of these capital allocators, since investors should be evaluated on how well they met their objectives (not just against the S&P 500's returns), and the objectives of institutions are difficult to measure from the outside.

So, instead of looking further into the institutional data to answer these questions, I began to look at data from retail traders. Is my audience, largely non-institutional investors, right?

Let's look at the data we have on where retail is positioned right now.

Retail Activity is at All-Time Highs

Several things came together all at once during the COVID-19 pandemic that changed retail flows, maybe forever. While the government was practicing "helicopter money" policies, they made flush with cash many people who truthfully didn't need it. For many of these folks, this was the first time they had a bunch of excess cash lying around. Why not invest it?

We created a new class of retail investor, alongside all the investors from pre-2020 that benefited from the massive rise in stock prices and valuations, who finally got the first group to listen about putting money away every month into that 401(K) and may have increased their own contributions.

The jump is easy to see when mapping retail flows into both stocks and ETFs.

This change was nowhere more pronounced than in young people; the under-40 crowd has never held a higher equity value in their portfolios, finally surpassing the 2021 peak this year.

Compare that to "smart money" allocations to the S&P 500. Looking at futures flows, which is far from the totality of holdings but does show the institutional directional bias, we can see that dealers (market makers), leveraged funds, and other traders with portfolios large enough that they are required to report ("whales") are not nearly as unified and definitely not nearly as long.

Asset managers are our closest comparison to the goals of most retail investors, so it makes sense that they are long as well, but their current net holdings sit below 2024 levels.

They Are Very Bullish...

Of the last 26 weeks, or half a year, retail traders have been net buyers in 23 of those weeks. This comes after a few small sell-offs into the market weakness we saw in February-April.

One piece of good news is that this is being done mostly with new money or stockpiled money that had been sitting in money market funds, etc. Current margin debt levels are growing, but not nearly at the same pace we see in previous bubbles.

During the dot-com bubble, investors ran up margin debts at 80% YoY, and above 70% in 2021. A figure of 35% today doesn't seem so bad by comparison.

...And They Like Options

One of the explanations I have for the reduced margin levels is the expansion of retail options activity.

Exclude all the options-related ETFs, as when they trade, they are technically counted in "smart money" stats, since institutional rules govern most funds. In fact, most of the covered call ETFs I cover either use in-house-built derivatives (can't get more institutional than that) or use off-exchange options like the very popular FLEX options offered by the OCC.

If we added the amount of retail money invested in options-based funds, it would be astronomical on top of this data from Citadel, showing its retail clients trading options in droves.

We are at multi-year highs in options volume, with the only other comparison being the meme stock bubble in January 2021, when GameStop (GME) et al. exploded.

Citadel also tracks the direction of these options. For the last 24 weeks, essentially since April, retail has been buying far more calls than puts and selling more puts than calls. The skew is wide, indicating just how bullish retail is currently.

It's not just trend chasing, necessarily, because institutional flows don't mirror this move at all. Institutional flows have taken the other side of the trade 8 out of the last 9 weeks and throughout the year. There were only a few times when their flows and retail flows aligned.

This makes sense to me, as it boils down to institutions hedging a lot more than retail does, and that shouldn't necessarily be a surprise to anyone.

Cash Is At Multi-Year Lows

Schwab (SCHW) clients have burned through their cash piles; this chart includes money markets but not cash equivalents, so we may be missing out on some investors who are holding T-Bill ETFs like SGOV over money markets, but I don't expect that blind spot to be the reason we're seeing the current trend in cash balances.

What's most interesting about this stat is that institutional traders are also historically low on cash, at least as of last month. They are sitting at levels they haven't seen since 2021 and 2013 before that.

Conclusion

Many institutional investors would likely conclude that retail investors are too bullish and are ripe to be shaken out by a prolonged crash. This is why they hedge.

Is the retail crowd the smart money now, with its near-permanent bullishness? After all, their portfolios are at record highs, they are plowing more money into the markets than ever before, and they are driving up options activity so much that they are no longer just an outlier but a player in the system to be respected.

The last two years have proven them right, and the crashes that we have seen since then have been fleeting and so not meaningful enough to be anything other than a dip to be bought by the retail masses.

I've learned that I'm less bullish than the general retail crowd; I already knew that anecdotally from comments, but the data confirms it. Perhaps I am overthinking the markets, just like the "smart money" institutions that get such a bad rap in my comments section.

It may serve me best to stop worrying and learn to love retail bullishness. Time will tell.

I'm not at that point yet and will continue to watch smart money flows.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: CME: Exchange Operator's Bullish Streak Should Continue As Market Data Segment Grows
data.description:
The Stock: A Major Exchange Operator With A Recent Earnings Beat This Week

There are always those few stocks that I keep coming back to for their interesting case. This time I am following up on major financial exchange operator CME Group Inc. (NASDAQ:CME) for my 4th edition of covering this company.

Since my very first article in May 2023, when I called it a Buy, this stock is up nearly +47%, justifying my longer-term bullish projection. Subsequently, the stock is up since my Dec. 2024 coverage, too, but slightly down since my July 2025 buy rating, showing it has pulled back slightly.

Common factors driving all of my past bullish calls include the firm's investment-grade credit rating, growing demand for the firm's data analytics solutions, and trading volumes.

For readers new to my articles, or to this company and sector, what we can see from its Seeking Alpha profile is that CME is a Chicago-based firm operating a market for trading options and futures, but also other solutions including market data services.

Since CME just released their latest quarterly earnings on Oct. 22nd, I've updated my prior research. Based on updated data, my thesis today argues that this stock should actually be upgraded to a Strong Buy this time.

Some key upside factors included in my rating worksheet below include several growth drivers, impressive margins, an investment-grade rating from Fitch and low D/E among peers, proven long-term dividend growth, and despite elevated fwd P/E valuation there is an additional +5% upside expected in the share price.

Rating Score: Strong Buy

Growth Drivers: Demand For Market Data and 24/7 Trading Key To Focus On

In this section, I'll touch on some points related to growth drivers, both macro-level and company-specific.

Global expansion is a growth driver, I think, and on that note I would mention that CME announced this month "its expansion into the Middle East with the opening of its Dubai office."

As a longtime analyst of business information systems, what particularly interested me about CME is one notable point from the recent quarterly results is that there seems to be increased top-line growth momentum in the firm's market data/information services practice:

So, that is a key area of growth I think investors should pay attention to. In fact, the quarterly presentation (pg. 6) highlighted that the market data practice was "driven by a growing subscriber count and the price increase that took effect in January."

What I like typically about a subscription-based model is that subscriptions signed this year can drive future revenues, assuming those clients stay on board.

Further, in terms of overall top-line growth, besides YoY revenue growth I would point out there is expected positive fwd revenue growth of +6.7%, very close to the sector average:

Additionally, from a "future" growth perspective, I think an upside driver could come from 24/7 trading, and recently the Investing.com news desk wrote:

The company is preparing for 24/7 cryptocurrency trading in 2026 and exploring tokenization opportunities with Google Cloud. Strategic initiatives also include expanding event-based contracts and focusing on retail strategy and market innovation.

From a macro view, there are some additional signs of growing demand for cryptocurrency trading, which of course needs a marketplace to function efficiently. For instance, Investopedia reported last spring that Israel-based trading platform eToro is filing for a U.S. IPO, as the company said a major part of their recent success is "an increase in crypto assets trading, which made up 38% of eToro’s commissions in 2024, more than doubling from 17% in 2023."

So, the above points are multiple growth factors without any significant offsetting downside factors, so for this reason I am going with a Strong Buy in this category.

Profitability: A Case of High Margins And Low Expense Growth

Next, let's talk about the topic of profitability.

Margins are key to this story, as I think they give a sense of a company's profit efficiency, in relation to peers.

Notable in that regard are some impressive headline margin numbers, such as CME's EBITDA margin of nearly +71% (vs sector avg of 21.5%) and a net income margin of nearly +59% (vs sector avg of 23%).

In my research, I did a comparison against 3 peers in the segment of financial exchanges and data, and these are NYSE parent company Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), Moody's (MCO), and Nasdaq (NDAQ):

From this comparison, data shows that CME easily beats these 3 peers on both profit margins. In case you're curious why I consider both of these margins, it's because the EBITDA excludes non-cash expenses like depreciation, so to me it is even more indicative of the core business profit efficiency.

Although these margins are trailing and not forward-looking, since they are based on data until now, I think it shows this firm has already proven itself with a model of profit efficiency, and this should boost its case as a serious company. Future earnings, of course, will depend on these margins maintained or improving even more.

In addition, CME's quarterly results (pg 6) showed some interesting data, in that total expenses have risen less than 1% on a YoY basis, which indicates the firm is keeping costs under control, which I think helps margins:

Lastly, on the topic of earnings, we cannot ignore what the consensus says as I think it could influence investor behavior, and in this case the consensus expects between 4-8% YoY EPS growth in each of the coming fiscal years, so this seems like a modestly positive forward view.

So, given that positive EPS view, the low expense growth, and the strong margins among peers, I am highly bullish in this category as well.

Balance Sheet & Credit Rating: Investment-Grade, and Very Low D/E vs Peers

In this section, I want to discuss some factors impacting balance sheet risk.

I think recently positive credit ratings from major agencies like Fitch and others adds a strong angle to a firm's risk profile, that investors should consider. For instance, back in February Fitch affirmed an investment-grade "AA-" rating, saying in its rating note that there are some positive future-looking factors and "views CME's predominantly long-term funding profile and demonstrated ability to raise long-duration debt favorably."

Next, I think it is worth comparing not just debt levels but debt in relation to equity, and comparing the debt/equity ratio vs. peers, here are the results:

In this data, we see CME has a D/E of only 0.13, which is extremely low and the lowest in this peer group, but also consider the trend line over 3 years shows that this D/E has not risen significantly in that time, indicating lower leverage risk that some peers.

Given the strong credit rating, and low D/E, and also lack of exposure to bad loans or commercial real estate (such is the case with many banks) this financial firm presents an interesting case of attractive balance sheet risk, and for this reason gets a buy rating from me in this category, however I would not overcomplicate this discussion today since the balance sheet is only one component of the overall rating.

Cashflow & Dividend Trends: Proven Growth In Both

Moving along, I am combining the discussion of dividend and cashflow trends.

As for dividends, for yield-driven investors I would point out the fwd dividend yield of +4% (vs. sector avg of 3%), but for growth-driven dividend investors this company can boast a proven track record of +9.5% dividend growth CAGR over 10 years, visualized as follows:

I think this shows evidence that CME has made a longer-term commitment to return capital to shareholders, even in the wake of the 2020 global pandemic when it did not seem to significantly cut its dividend, a sign it could be better equipped to withstand such economic shocks in the future too, than some firms and sectors that I've seen (such as some hotels and airlines) which had to cut dividends in the wake of the pandemic. As an example, Delta Airlines (DAL) had no dividends in 2021 and 2022.

Cashflow trends need to support the dividend, however, and what I want to point out in that regard when it comes to CME is its core operating cashflow growth was +17.5% (vs sector avg of 11.3%), while expected forward growth is +8.55% (slightly behind the sector avg). Both of these are positive factors to consider in the cashflow picture, since operating cashflow reflects the core business and not financing or investing.

Again, these points above only add to the already bullish sentiment in this article up until now.

Valuation & Share Price: A 5% Further Price Appreciation Expected, In Wake of Bullish Stock Direction

Coming towards the closing topic in this article, let's get into the realm of valuation and share price.

A forward-looking valuation metric worth considering is the fwd P/E, in this case both the non-GAAP and GAAP-based multiple is around 26x, so let's use that.

Both are significantly overvalued vs the sector average, and in addition we can see from momentum data that CME has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) in the last 6 months, in terms of price return.

Additionally, for a considerable time, this stock has been trading above its 200-day SMA ever since last year:

So, this is a good example where the stock has elevated valuation, momentum vs the S&P 500, and is trading above average, so it seems they all align this time and indicate strong market confidence about the future potential of this company.

Considering the evidence I presented including strong profit margins, a positive future EPS growth consensus, and low leverage risk, I think this market bullishness is more than justified. However, in order to buy or hold this stock at this time, there should also be adequate further upside in the share price, so on that topic I made this price target worksheet:

In the worksheet above, the data shows I have a price target on this stock of $279.64, while the avg Wall Street consensus target is $282.59, and on average both expect around a +5% upside vs the last closing price.

With that said, I think a 5% upside is relatively low for a new buyer, so in this category it moves the needle more into the hold column, however keep in mind this is only one factor in my overall rating and not decisive by itself.

Conclusion: More Bullish Than Consensus

In conclusion, my rating today of a Strong Buy is even more bullish than the consensus from analysts, Wall Street, and the quant system.

Despite a low price upside forecast, the other factors discussed today more than offset that factor and drive this stock into the strong buy category, including income statement, dividend, cashflow, and balance sheet factors.

However, it is fair to also consider a risk that could challenge my confident outlook, and one that has not yet been included in this article is that a core business of this firm's relies on trading volumes in the options and futures markets, since CME makes money on that volume, to put it simply. This volume can continually fluctuate, and we can visualize that in the chart below from CME:

Because of such volatility, I think investors should expect fluctuation in future revenue in that part of the business, but a mitigating factor overall is that CME has other business segments too, such as the market data segment and subscription-driven businesses, thereby diversifying the future revenue picture.

Therefore, my call-to-action is to continue betting on exchanges like this with a combination strong fundamentals and continued market momentum.

data.title: Quantum Computing: I'm Not Buying This Massive Dip Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
The Quantum Computing Dip Came

It's been an extremely tough week for all quantum computing stocks out there, with most of them being down over 30% on average (although they're still up a lot on a YoY basis):

One of the QC names that I cover on Seeking Alpha is Quantum Computing Inc. (NASDAQ:QUBT), which fell by 42% off its 52-week high, and it's currently trading close to its 200-day simple moving average, visually begging growth investors to step in and buy a couple of weeks before the firm's fiscal Q3 2025 earnings release (based on Seeking Alpha's data, QUBT reports on 11/10/2025, post-market).

Why I'm Not Buying At QUBT's 200-Day MA Line?

While it might seem tempting for a dip buyer to pile back in close to the well-known moving average, which is considered to be a kind of resistance line for any technical analyst, I believe we should be mindful to assess what's going on in the industry and within the firm firsthand.

Last time I looked at QUBT, it was trading at $19.73 per share, and I wrote that the prevailing "wishful thinking" surrounding the stock at the time was setting up a dangerous condition for newcomers. QUBT has fallen by over 24% since then, and I think there might come an even lower correction in the next weeks: While the broader quantum computing industry is undoubtedly exciting, it's a catalyst for mega-cap players like Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) and Nvidia (NVDA), who are developing their own quantum computing solutions at a larger scale, targeting a wider TAM eventually.

QUBT remains a small player, which is dramatically overpriced even after the stock price meltdown. The firm brought in $61,000 in sales for Q2, missing the consensus estimates of ~$0.1 million, and showing a massive 66.67% YoY decline. I see no tangible top-line impact from their past plans and expectations. Quantum Computing is going through a significant cash burn, having spent ~$10.2 million on OPEX in Q2 (vs. -$5.3 million last year), because of employee-based expenses. I think there will be even more intensive spending ahead, with more risks to the top-line growth.

QUBT and its direct peers need to hire highly-qualified staff in an environment where everyone starts to understand that QC tech is real, and that it has tremendous growth opportunities in the coming years. When I write "everyone", I primarily mean large and mega-cap tech firms like Google, Nvidia, IBM (IBM), Microsoft (MSFT), you name it. They are willing and are capable of investing in QC development more than QUBT, Rigetti (RGTI), Argit (ARQQ) are worth. But what exacerbates the whole forward growth picture for QUBT and its direct peers is the fact that mega caps have already been developing their proprietary QC tech for the past years. And we can see the initial successful results.

For example, Google has just released its new Quantum Echoes algorithm within the firm's quantum computing chip, known as Willow, reaching "first-ever verifiable quantum advantage":

This new algorithm can explain interactions between atoms in a molecule using nuclear magnetic resonance, paving a path towards potential future uses in drug discovery and materials science. And the result is verifiable, meaning its outcome can be repeated by other quantum computers or confirmed by experiments. This breakthrough is a significant step toward the first real-world application of quantum computing, and we're excited to see where it leads.

Source: Seeking Alpha News

Another mega, which is actively developing its in-house quantum computing solutions, is Nvidia, with its special focus on "error correction, simulations of qubit designs, and circuit compilation optimization".

While not directly building quantum hardware in the same way as QUBT does, Nvidia's influence and the critical tools it's building are essential for the entire quantum ecosystem, and I think it can potentially mean future collaborations with other large caps that partially specialize in hardware building.

What I want to say with all the above discussion is that it's getting extremely hard to compete against firms with bottomless balance sheets.

Quantum Computing Inc. is probably one of the best-capitalized entities in the niche compared to RGTI, QUBT, ARQQ, and others, and they've been quite active in attracting new capital after the stock price made over 3,000% in a matter of months.

During Q1 2025, QUBT raised ~$93.6 million through common stock offerings. Then in Q2, they raised another $200 million financing round. Most recently, in October 2025, we saw them raising ~$750 million in gross proceeds through entering into securities purchase agreements for an oversubscribed private placement of ~37.18 million shares (their pro-forma cash position should be ~$1.55 billion, based on SA data). So, effectively, QUBT is trading at ~2.15x pro-forma-based price-to-book, which is the cheapest ratio among direct peers.

With this amount of cash on the books, QUBT should now be ready for a "fully funded commercialization, pursue strategic acquisitions, establish volume production capabilities". But the current consensus estimates still see only $10.2 million in annual sales in 2 years - it's an extremely low figure for all the funding that QUBT secured.

Given that the competition is rising in the niche, and major players are ready to push it further, I believe that the execution risks at QUBT are very high to date. Yes, it's well-capitalized after all the financing rounds it recently held, but the integration of quantum machines with nano-photonic chips and the enhancement of performance are milestones targeted over the next 2-3 years, and this projection can be further postponed if better tech alternatives from large-caps' labs come during this period.

QUBT's Upcoming Q3 Earnings Release

With companies like Quantum Computing Inc., EPS and revenues don't matter as much as the management's commentary and its execution of the past plans and strategies.

QUBT's Arizona Quantum Photonic Chip Foundry has been scaling, and the CEO noted last time that they were "targeting a significant revenue contribution in the next 12 to 18 months" as they keep ramping, so it's going to be the most critical thing to monitor this quarter.

Another point of interest should come from the management's execution of QUBT's integration of its Quantum machines with its nanophotonic chips, which should theoretically "reduce size, weight, power, and also the cost of [their] Quantum machine", boosting the overall performance.

In terms of financials, I'd monitor QUBT's OPEX expansion. They were supposed to start hiring more personnel, so I'd expect to see a massive boost in costs, with likely little addition to the top line (for now). The orders for their quantum systems and foundry services should pick up, and we saw some promising signs of early demand last quarter. But it didn't translate to a major sales boost in Q2, and I don't think Q3 will look much different.

The EPS and sales consensus for Q3 are -$0.06 and $116.67 thousand, respectively, and while I agree that the top line can come in closer to that amount, I think that because of the expected OPEX boosting, the EPS will be much deeper in the red. We'll see how the market reacts.

Final Words

I don't question QUBT's tech advantage over others, as there are still a lot of unknown facts. The demand side and the past orders look promising indeed, and QUBT has arguably the best balance sheet among direct peers to date. But still, the execution risks are high, and the competition environment is getting more complex with all the new updates from Google, Nvidia, and other large-cap players.

The odds of seeing earnings revisions turn negative and the breakeven consensus drifting further away from QUBT are high, in my opinion. The company's path to sustainable profitability is still long and uncertain, and I can't rate it as a "buy," even after seeing it dip by over 40% off its high.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Vita Coco: Healthy Business And Growth Potential
data.description:
I first wrote about The Vita Coco Company, Inc. (NASDAQ:COCO) in January 2024, where I was bullish on the company because of its consistency at beating earnings, its frontrunner position in coconut water, and its long-term growth potential through diversifying its product line and expanding internationally. Since then, the stock has rewarded investors with returns of 93.53%. The company continued with its upward-trending financials in FY2024 and into the FH 2025, with strong cash flow and no debt, but the valuation has become less attractive, particularly relative to peers I compared it with previously. Although the company is pricier, for long-term investors, I believe this is still a strong company interested in long-term investing. It will be releasing its Q3 2025 earnings results on 29 October and increased its FY2025 guideline in Q2 2025.

Company Update

Since my previous article, Vita Coco ended on a strong FY 2024 and completed the FH 2025 on an upward performance trend with a YoY sales increase of 17% to $300 million. It has increased its FY 2025 guidance and will be announcing its Q3 2025 earnings at the end of this month. Most of its topline growth has come from more customers buying its core coconut water products. An ongoing challenge for the company is its freight and raw material costs, particularly as competition increases from larger beverage industry players.

Vita Coco's growth is being driven by a few factors. The main one is still its core coconut water line, which continues to gain shelf space and new retail partners. Multipacks and larger formats are helping drive repeat purchases and higher volumes. The company is also pushing newer products that aim to reach shoppers looking for natural hydration and protein options. If we look internationally, sales are increasing alongside distribution expansion, particularly in Europe. In the table below we can see a 40% YoY increase in sales in FH 2025 for Vita Coco Coconut Water and a 10% increase for its Private label.

While we should keep a cautious eye on the potential impact of increasing freight costs and the potential tariff threat, at the same time, Vita Coco has shown that its brand strength across a larger variety of product categories has allowed for higher prices while maintaining demand.

Financial Overview

Since going public, Vita Coco has kept growing year after year. Revenue has grown from $283.9 million in FY 2019 to $559.9 million in the trailing twelve months (TTM). At the same time, we have noticed that gross profit has more than doubled over the same period to $202.2 million TTM. The company has managed to expand its margins despite inflation and freight costs, although we should be aware that the management is feeling the cost pressure as indicated in Q2 2025.

Vita Coco's net income has increased consistently over the last three financial years and is on its way to continuing this trend in FY 2025. It has increased to $64.4 million TTM from $7.8 million in FY2022. This is a good indication for potential investors that the business can remain and grow in profitability.

We can see that Vita Coco's levered free cash flow has been inconsistent. Vita Coco's free cash flow has dropped sharply from about $30.1 million last year to $0.9 million. The main reason is higher inventory and receivables as the company built up stock ahead of peak demand and expanded distribution. While the net income of the business increased, the cash flow growth was less because it is within the company's working capital.

In the table below, we can see that the company has a healthy balance sheet with $167 million in cash and barely any debt, at $2.94 million. This gives it flexibility if costs increase or if it wants to invest in the business. It has a current ratio of 3.51, which indicates that it can cover its short-term liabilities and gives investors an indication that it can handle some volatility if the market were to weaken.

Valuation

Vita Coco's stock price has increased by 93.54% since I last wrote about it. Interestingly enough, the valuation numbers and comparison to some of the same beverage peers indicate that Vita Coco's valuation remains equally attractive. Vita Coco's valuation has increased only slightly. The stock now has a forward (FWD) price-to-earnings (P/E) of 34.03, which is higher than its five-year average. As was previously the case, the beverage giants, namely The Coca-Cola Company (KO), which is at 22.53, and PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP), which is at 23.33, both have lower valuations. Finally, its valuation also remains just under that of Monster Beverage Corporation (MNST), which now has a forward P/E of 35.20. This valuation is actually similar to the valuation in my previous article. The higher valuation is connected to Vita Coco's fast growth rate and the fact that its profitability has continued to improve. If we look at the FWD EV-to-sales basis, Vita Coco sits at 3.62, compared to Monster at 8.30 and Zevia PBC (ZVIA) at 0.76. While Zevia appears undervalued, it has yet to generate positive earnings, which is a risk investors need to consider. To me, that puts Vita Coco in the middle of the pack. While it isn't cheap, it isn't too expensive relative to its strong financial performance. The company has a healthy balance sheet, positive cash flow, and steady category growth, which makes the valuation still feel acceptable.

Risks

Choosing to invest in this stock is not without its risks. First, there is cost pressure that can have a negative impact on the company's ability to generate profit. Management has already mentioned the issue in its Q2 2025 earnings call, mentioning the increase in freight charges and higher input costs. Another risk is that the company's distribution is still relatively small compared to larger competitors. This means the company's sales are prone to more fluctuations depending on what these retailers decide to do with their inventory. Another issue is the increase in competition from large beverage companies that have entered the coconut-water space. These companies have economies of scale and distribution reach much greater than that of Vita Coco. Lastly, most of the company's revenue is dependent on a steady supply of coconuts.

Final Thoughts

Vita Coco's stock has increased by 93.53% since my last article. The increase means that although the company is still producing upward-trending results, its valuation is pricier. I still see growth potential through the company's ability to grow internationally, expand its product lines and long-term growth trends within the healthier beverage segment. It also has no debt and strong cash flow, and the management team appears very proactive in growing the business. Therefore, I remain cautiously bullish for investors who are long-term oriented. The company is releasing its Q3 2025 results on 29 October.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 242: $24,200 Allocated, $2,671.61 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was a much needed reversal as the market recovered from the dramatic sell-off on Friday over tensions between China and the U.S. We started the week with a rally as President Trump softened his tone over the weekend, which created a bit less uncertainty. Q3 earnings season is finally here, and the financials led the way as the large money centers such as JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Citigroup (C) produced large top and bottom line beats. We had a new deal announced by OpenAI as they will be contracting 10 gigawatts of chips from Broadcom (AVGO) bringing their total contracted chip amount to 26 gigawatts between AVGO, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). We got some regional bank jitters as there were some disclosures about bad loans from some smaller banks, such as Zions Bancorp (ZION) hit the market, but I am not to worried about a few of the smaller regionals. Ultimately the S&P 500 increased 1.7%, while the Nasdaq rose 2.1% this week, which helped the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. We’re heading into the heart of earnings season with companies such as Netflix (NFLX), Tesla (TSLA), and International Business Machines (IBM) kicking off technology, then the megacaps starting to report the following week. I am optimistic that earnings season is going to be strong and that the next Fed meeting will lead to another cut and more stability in monetary policy.

The bleeding stopped as President Trump calmed the markets a bit, which led to the market slightly rebounding this week. After allocating $24,200 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 242 at $31,414.98. The account's profitability increased by 4.14% ($287.15) to $7,214.98, which is a return on invested capital of 29.81%. This week I added to my positions in Starwood Property Trust (STWD), Verizon (VZ), and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $44.91 of dividend income in week 242. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $21.91 (0.83%) to $2,671.61. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,046.90 of income since its inception, which is 20.85% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. As I indicated last week, I don’t believe the tensions between China and the U.S. will be as dramatic as they were in April and that we could see these issues fade away as we get earnings reports from the largest companies in the world while the Fed continues to cut rates. I am excited for the next several weeks and seeing how these companies are positioned to finish out 2025. I am bullish on the U.S. economy and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio going forward.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

Thankfully the markets reversed a bit this week, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio’s profitability rebounded by 4.14% ($287.15) to $7,215.98 after last week’s debacle. I started this account with $100 and added $100 each week with the intention of building an income-producing portfolio from the ground up. My focus was to build a portfolio that would mitigate risk while generating recurring income. I wanted capital appreciation to be a secondary goal, as I was not focused on replicating or trying to exceed the market's return. I am very happy with the results since it has been a very long time since the account value was under the amount of capital that was invested. Currently, I have invested $24,200, the account is at $31,414.98, and it is generating $2,671.61 in forward income, which is an 8.5% portfolio yield. I am excited to see where this ends up at the end of 2025, as I feel the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will continue to do well.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $957.66 (35.85%)
  • Equities $594.60 (22.26%)
  • CEFs $433.02 (16.21%)
  • REITs $427.48 (16%)
  • BDCs $258.84 (9.69%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 21 weeks in 2025, $1,867.22 of dividend income was produced, which is 115.04% of the total income from 2024. There were 627 positions that paid a dividend, which is 88.43% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $44.91 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $44.46. This is an increase of $13.24 (42.43%) YoY, and there have been 4 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I’m really excited for the rest of September to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

We’re halfway through October and I have already generated $126.68 in dividend income. The first month in each quarter has been exceptionally strong in 2025 and I have a feeling that I am going to have my largest month of income being generated this month. 2025 is coming down the home stretch and I have already generated more income than in 2024 with 2 and a half months to go. I am excited to see if I can break $215 this month and what that will mean for November and December. It’s going to be exciting to see when the next milestones of generating $225 and $250 of income occur.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 45 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $587.01 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding to REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. This week I added to CEFs, but ETFs are still 20.6% of the portfolio. I am going to work and get this down in the future. Individual equities now represent 38.89% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.26% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.11% of the portfolio and generate 77.74% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

I am going to have to accept the fact that Altria Group (MO) is going to be over 4% of the portfolio for quite some time. I’m just very surprised because at this point it will be more than 2 years since I have added to it, and it feels like it will still be the largest position in the portfolio. I think that there is an opportunity to add more to some of these positions and I am glad that Verizon (VZ) is representing less of the portfolio, as it makes me feel less guilty about adding to it. One of the keys to this portfolio’s success has been the level of diversification. Eventually, the top 3 positions will get smaller, and I will feel more comfortable allocating capital toward them. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

I added to VZ, STWD, and PDI this week which changed the dynamics of the top-10 a bit. There were also some dividends that were paid. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,822.12, and they finished the week with a value of $9,204.98. This is an ROI of $2,382.86 (34.93%). There has been $1,391.68 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 20.40% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $737.53 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.88%. These positions represent 29.30% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.61% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 242 Additions

This week I added to my positions in Starwood Property Trust (STWD), Verizon (VZ), and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI).

Starwood Property Trust

  • Shares of STWD fell from over $20 down to around $18.50 which pushed the yield past 10% so I added to my position
  • I think Barry Sternlicht (STWD CEO) is one of the best CEOs in the REIT sector and I am excited for their Q3 earnings call
  • I think that STWD is undervalued, as it just fell below its book value of $18.80 and this is a REIT that I want to own more of

Verizon

  • I think that VZ under $40 is a steal as the yield has reached almost 7%
  • I think too many people are scared of the debt load on VZ’s balance sheet, yet they are likely overlooking the fact that in the trailing twelve months (TTM) has generated $49.92 billion in EBITDA.
  • As rates come down, yield-starved investors will be hunting for yield, and I believe VZ will look more attractive as the risk-free rate of return declines.

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund

  • PDI recently dipped from $20 to $18 which pushed the yield past 14%, so I added to my position
  • I believe that PDI’s NAV will expand as the Fed cuts rates and bonds in its portfolio become more valuable
  • I think that PDI’s distribution has proven that it can be resilient in the worst of times and that the income will continue to hit shareholders accounts as rates decline

Week 243 Gameplan

Next week I plan on adding to my position in CTO Realty Growth (CTO)

Conclusion

October is shaping up to be a strong month for dividend income, and the S-curve is in full effect. When the market dips I don’t panic, I allocate capital because I have a long-term mindset. This week China-U.S. tensions may have sent the market spiraling down but it allowed me to add to STWD and PDI around $18.50 an VZ around $40. I will take those deals all day as I was able to lock in strong yields on cost at these levels. With the way October is shaping up I could reach $215 of dividend income which could set the stage for a very big Q4. Big Tech is getting ready to report, and we have a Fed meeting coming up which I believe will allow the market to climb higher into the end of the year. No matter what happens, I will continue to get excited to allocate capital each week to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. Please leave all your comments and suggestions in the comment section below as I try to interact with everyone throughout the week.

data.title: Whitecap Resources: Strong Production In Q3, Softer Energy Prices
data.description:
Overview

Whitecap Resources (TSX:WCP:CA)(OTCPK:WCPRF) is a Canadian oil and natural gas producer that has graduated to a large-cap producer following its recent acquisition of Veren. That acquisition closed in May of this year.

The company has a stellar operating history, which has managed to grow its production on an absolute level and on a per-share basis over the last few years and over the long term. Whitecap has now raised its production guidance for 2025 to 305,000 boe/d and the current production volume is around 370,000 boe/d.

This is a company I have covered quarterly for a few years, and my prior articles can be found here. Whitecap has also been in my portfolio a couple of times over the last few years. The second holding period in 2025 turned out to be short-lived after the stock price went from very oversold in April to more fairly valued just a few months later.

Today's article will focus on the Q3 2025 result that was released yesterday after the close. Whitecap will host a webcast today at 11 am ET, which can be watched here.

The total return of Whitecap has been modest this year, up 4% in U.S. dollar terms. However, when we consider that the WTI price has declined by as much as 15% and many energy companies have struggled, this is a relatively decent stock price performance by Whitecap in 2025.

Q3 2025 Result

Q3 2025 was the first full quarter including the Veren assets. Last quarter was a little bit messy with the acquisition closing mid-quarter, so some quarter-over-quarter comparisons are less useful now. Whitecap reported a strong production of 374,623 boe/d in Q3, with 61% liquids. The production per share in Q3 was 4.7% higher than Q3 last year.

The most recent quarter was yet another quarter with relatively soft energy prices. Whitecap reported a total realized price of C$48.17/boe in the most recent quarter, down 6.5% compared to Q2 2025. The crude oil price was marginally higher quarter-over-quarter, while the natural gas price decreased by as much as 29.2% quarter-over-quarter, which had an outsized impact on the total realized price.

A strong production and softer energy prices led to a decent funds flow of C$897M in Q3 2025. The funds flow per share was C$0.73, which was a 4% decline compared to the prior quarter, and free funds flow per share was C$0.29, down 11% compared to Q2. The company still has the dividend well covered.

Whitecap got a larger amount of debt with the Veren acquisition and has recently issued some more senior notes at an attractive interest rate of 3.761%. The net debt was C$3.3B in the most recent quarter, up marginally compared to Q2.

If we annualize the Q3 2025 funds flow, the net debt to funds flow comes to 0.9, which is a manageable leverage ratio for this investment grade rated company, using what at least I consider to be softer energy prices.

In the table below, we can see some figures on a C$/boe basis for Q3 compared to Q3 last year. Royalties and opex are down, while transportation expenses have increased. The lower energy prices might explain some of the decreases compared to Q3 2024, but royalties on the Veren assets have been lower. Interest expenses have also decreased marginally, and the lower interest rate in the market has offset the higher debt load per production unit. Overall, the differences compared to last year are relatively small.

Conclusion

Whitecap is a high-quality oil & natural gas producer. The company has a stellar operating history, a consistent growth trajectory, and plenty of reserves.

Whether Whitecap is cheap or not probably depends on energy prices and what multiple is appropriate for a company of this size and quality. Compared to larger Canadian peers, the forward-looking EV to EBITDA margin is still somewhat lower at 4.5. The dividend yield of 7.1% is also relatively attractive for a company of its size. So, there is likely some re-rate potential here over time.

As illustrated in figure 2, Whitecap has traded marginally higher while the WTI price has declined 15% this year, which has not improved the valuation using strip prices. With that said, I think current energy prices are on the low side, and it would not be overly aggressive to consider a WTI price 15-20% higher as a mid-cycle price, which would leave plenty of upside for Whitecap.

Based on the 2026 guidance that was released yesterday, the company is projecting a free cash flow yield of 10% against the market cap and 8% against the enterprise value.

However, I would argue that there are many oil & natural gas producers that are trading with low earnings multiples and relatively high dividend yields. So, I remain neutral on Whitecap for the time being. I would need to see a 10-15% share price decline for me to be interested in adding Whitecap to the portfolio, which I think we are unlikely to see without more downside in energy prices.
